The **Welsh** ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Cymry*) are an [ethnic group](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group") and [nation](/wiki/Nation "Nation") native to [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") who share a common ancestry, [history](/wiki/History_of_Wales "History of Wales") and [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Wales "Culture of Wales").[[12]](#cite_note-12) Wales is one of the four [countries of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom"). The majority of people living in Wales are [British citizens](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law").[[13]](#cite_note-Stats_1-13)

In Wales, the [Welsh language](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Cymraeg*) is protected by law.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Welsh remains the predominant language in many parts of Wales, particularly in [North Wales](/wiki/North_Wales "North Wales") and parts of [West Wales](/wiki/West_Wales "West Wales"), though English is the predominant language in [South Wales](/wiki/South_Wales "South Wales"). The Welsh language is also taught in schools in Wales; and, even in regions of Wales in which Welsh people predominantly speak English on a daily basis, the Welsh language is spoken at home among family or in informal settings, with Welsh speakers often engaging in [code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching") and [translanguaging](/wiki/Translanguaging "Translanguaging"). In the English-speaking areas of Wales, many Welsh people are [bilingually fluent](/wiki/Multilingualism "Multilingualism") or semi-fluent in the Welsh language or, to varying degrees, capable of speaking or understanding the language at limited or conversational [proficiency](/wiki/Language_proficiency "Language proficiency") levels. The Welsh language is descended from Brythonic, spoken across Britain since before the [Roman invasion](/wiki/Caesar%27s_invasions_of_Britain "Caesar's invasions of Britain").

In 2016, an analysis of the geography of [Welsh surnames](/wiki/Welsh_surnames "Welsh surnames") commissioned by the [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government") found that 718,000 people (nearly 35% of the Welsh population) have a family name of Welsh origin, compared with 5.3% in the rest of the United Kingdom, 4.7% in New Zealand, 4.1% in Australia, and 3.8% in the United States, with an estimated 16.3 million people in the countries studied having at least partial Welsh ancestry.[[15]](#cite_note-Welsh_names-15) Over 300,000 Welsh people live in [London](/wiki/London "London").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

See also: [Etymology of Wales](/wiki/Etymology_of_Wales "Etymology of Wales") and [Cymru](/wiki/Cymru "Cymru")

The names "Wales" and "Welsh" are modern descendants of the Anglo-Saxon word *[wealh](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/wealh "wikt:wealh")*, a descendant of the [Proto-Germanic](/wiki/Proto-Germanic_language "Proto-Germanic language") word *[walhaz](/wiki/Walhaz "Walhaz")*, which was derived from the name of the [Gaulish people](/wiki/Gauls "Gauls") known to the Romans as [Volcae](/wiki/Volcae "Volcae") and which came to refer indiscriminately to inhabitants of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire").[[17]](#cite_note-17) The [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English")-speaking [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") came to use the term to refer to the [Britons](/wiki/Britons_(historical) "Britons (historical)") in particular. As the Britons' territories shrank, the term came ultimately to be applied to a smaller group of people, and the plural form of *Wealh*, *Wēalas*, evolved into the name for the territory that best maintained cultural continuity with pre-Anglo-Saxon Britain: Wales.[[18]](#cite_note-Davies_1994_71-18) The modern names for various [Romance-speaking](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") people in [Continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe") (e.g. [Wallonia](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia"), [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia"), *[Valais](/wiki/Valais "Valais")*, [Vlachs](/wiki/Vlachs "Vlachs"), and *Włochy*, the [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") name for Italy) have a similar etymology.[[18]](#cite_note-Davies_1994_71-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-Tolkien_1-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Gilleland-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Davies_1994_69-22)

The modern Welsh name for themselves is *Cymry* (plural) (singular: *Cymro* [m] and *Cymraes* [f]), and *Cymru* is the Welsh name for Wales. These words (both of which are pronounced [[ˈkəmrɨ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Welsh "Help:IPA/Welsh") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Cymru.ogg "File:Cymru.ogg")) are descended from the [Brythonic](/wiki/British_language_(Celtic) "British language (Celtic)") word *kombrogi*, meaning "fellow-countrymen".[[22]](#cite_note-Davies_1994_69-22) Thus, they carry a sense of "land of fellow-countrymen", "our country", and notions of fraternity. The use of the word *Cymry* as a self-designation derives from the [post-Roman Era](/wiki/Sub-Roman_Britain "Sub-Roman Britain") relationship of the Welsh with the Brythonic-speaking peoples of northern England and southern Scotland, the peoples of "[Yr Hen Ogledd](/wiki/Hen_Ogledd "Hen Ogledd")" (English: The Old North).[[23]](#cite_note-23) The word came into use as a self-description probably before the 7th century.[[24]](#cite_note-24)
It is attested in a praise poem to [Cadwallon ap Cadfan](/wiki/Cadwallon_ap_Cadfan "Cadwallon ap Cadfan") (*Moliant Cadwallon*,[[25]](#cite_note-25) by Afan Ferddig) c. 633.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In [Welsh literature](/wiki/Literature_of_Wales_(Welsh_language) "Literature of Wales (Welsh language)"), the word *Cymry* was used throughout the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") to describe the Welsh, though the older, more generic term *Brythoniaid* continued to be used to describe any of the [Britonnic peoples](/wiki/Britons_(historical) "Britons (historical)"), including the Welsh, and was the more common literary term until c. 1100. Thereafter *Cymry* prevailed as a reference to the Welsh. Until c. 1560 the word was spelt *Kymry* or *Cymry*, regardless of whether it referred to the people or their homeland.[[22]](#cite_note-Davies_1994_69-22)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of Wales](/wiki/History_of_Wales "History of Wales") and [Genetic history of the British Isles](/wiki/Genetic_history_of_the_British_Isles "Genetic history of the British Isles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r_at_Cardiff_City_Hall.jpg/250px-Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r_at_Cardiff_City_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r_at_Cardiff_City_Hall.jpg)

[Owain Glyndŵr](/wiki/Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r "Owain Glyndŵr") was proclaimed [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales") by his supporters on 16 September 1400. He was the last native Welsh person to hold the title.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Enrique_VII_de_Inglaterra%2C_por_un_artista_an%C3%B3nimo.jpg/250px-Enrique_VII_de_Inglaterra%2C_por_un_artista_an%C3%B3nimo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Enrique_VII_de_Inglaterra,_por_un_artista_an%C3%B3nimo.jpg)

[King Henry VII](/wiki/Henry_VII_of_England "Henry VII of England"), the founder of the royal house of [Tudor](/wiki/House_of_Tudor "House of Tudor")

During their [time in Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain"), the ancient [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") encountered tribes in present-day Wales that they called the [Ordovices](/wiki/Ordovices "Ordovices"), the [Demetae](/wiki/Demetae "Demetae"), the [Silures](/wiki/Silures "Silures") and the [Deceangli](/wiki/Deceangli "Deceangli").[[27]](#cite_note-27) The people of what is now Wales were not distinguished from the rest of the peoples of southern Britain; all were called [Britons](/wiki/Britons_(historical) "Britons (historical)") and spoke [Common Brittonic](/wiki/Common_Brittonic "Common Brittonic"), a [Celtic language](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages").[[28]](#cite_note-BBC_Hist-28) This language, and Celtic culture more generally, seems to have arrived in Britain during the [Iron Age](/wiki/British_Iron_Age "British Iron Age"), though some archaeologists argue that there is no evidence for large-scale [Iron Age](/wiki/British_Iron_Age "British Iron Age") migrations into Great Britain,[[29]](#cite_note-cunliffe-29) in which case the Celticisation of Britain would have occurred through cultural diffusion.

Most people in Wales today regard themselves as [modern Celts](/wiki/Modern_Celts "Modern Celts"), claiming a heritage back to the Iron Age tribes. When the [Roman legions departed Britain](/wiki/End_of_Roman_rule_in_Britain "End of Roman rule in Britain") around 400, a [Romano-British](/wiki/Romano-British "Romano-British") culture remained in the areas the Romans had settled, and the pre-Roman cultures in others.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The people in what is now Wales continued to speak [Common Brittonic](/wiki/Common_Brittonic "Common Brittonic") with significant influence from [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"), as did people in other areas of western and northern Britain; this language eventually evolved into [Old Welsh](/wiki/Old_Welsh "Old Welsh"). The surviving poem "[Y Gododdin](/wiki/Y_Gododdin "Y Gododdin")" is in early Welsh and refers to the [British](/wiki/Britons_(historical) "Britons (historical)") kingdom of [Gododdin](/wiki/Gododdin "Gododdin") with a capital at [Din Eidyn](/wiki/Din_Eidyn "Din Eidyn") ([Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh")) and extending from the area of [Stirling](/wiki/Stirling "Stirling") to the Tyne.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) [Offa's Dyke](/wiki/Offa%27s_Dyke "Offa's Dyke") was erected in the mid-8th century, forming a barrier between Wales and [Mercia](/wiki/Mercia "Mercia").

The process whereby the indigenous population of Wales came to think of themselves as "Welsh" (a name applied to them by [Anglo-Saxon settlers](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons")) is not clear. There is plenty of evidence of the use of the term *Brythoniaid* (Britons); meanwhile, the earliest use of the word *Kymry* (referring not to the people but to the land—and possibly to northern Britain in addition to Wales) is found in a poem c. 633. The name of the region in northern England now known as [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria") is derived from the same root.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Only gradually did *Cymru* (the land) and *Cymry* (the people) come to supplant *Brython*. Although the Welsh language was certainly used at the time, [Gwyn A. Williams](/wiki/Gwyn_A._Williams "Gwyn A. Williams") argues that even at the time of the erection of Offa's Dyke, the people to its west saw themselves as Roman, citing the number of Latin inscriptions still being made into the 8th century.[[34]](#cite_note-34) However, it is unclear whether such inscriptions reveal a general or normative use of Latin as a marker of identity or its selective use by the early [Christian Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church").

There was immigration to Wales after the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"), and several [Normans](/wiki/Normans "Normans") encouraged [immigration](/wiki/Flemish_settlement_in_Pembrokeshire "Flemish settlement in Pembrokeshire") to their new lands; the [Landsker Line](/wiki/Landsker_Line "Landsker Line") dividing the [Pembrokeshire](/wiki/Pembrokeshire "Pembrokeshire") "Englishry" and "Welshry" is still detectable today.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The terms Englishry and Welshry are used similarly about [Gower](/wiki/Gower_Peninsula "Gower Peninsula").[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Genetic studies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Genetic studies")]

| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **only references primary sources**. Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability").  *Find sources:* ["Welsh people"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Welsh+people%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Welsh+people%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Welsh+people%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Welsh+people%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Welsh+people%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Welsh+people%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(April 2023)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Recent research on ancient DNA has concluded that much of Britain's Neolithic population was replaced by [Beaker people](/wiki/Bell_Beaker_Culture "Bell Beaker Culture") in the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age").[[37]](#cite_note-Olalde_et_al.-37) The British groups encountered by the Romans were thus largely descended from these Beaker populations.

The post-Roman period saw a significant alteration in the genetic makeup of southern Britain due to the arrival of the Anglo-Saxons; however, historical evidence suggests that Wales was little affected by these migrations. A study published in 2016 compared samples from modern Britain and Ireland with DNA found in skeletons from Iron Age, Roman and Anglo-Saxon era Yorkshire. The study found that most of the Iron Age and Roman era Britons showed strong similarities with both each other and modern-day Welsh populations, while modern southern and eastern English groups were closer to a later Anglo-Saxon burial.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Another study, using Iron Age and Anglo-Saxon samples from Cambridgeshire, concluded that modern Welsh people carry a 30% genetic contribution from Anglo-Saxon settlers in the post-Roman period; however, this could have been brought about due to later migration from England into Wales.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

A third study, published in 2020 and based on Viking era data from across Europe, suggested that the Welsh trace, on average, 58% of their ancestry to the Brittonic people, up to 22% from a Danish-like source interpreted as largely representing the Anglo-Saxons, 3% from Norwegian Vikings, and 13% from further south in Europe such as [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), to a lesser extent, [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") and can possibly be related to French immigration during the Norman period.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

A 2015 genetic survey of modern British population groups found a distinct genetic difference between those from northern and southern Wales, which was interpreted as the legacy of [Little England beyond Wales](/wiki/Little_England_beyond_Wales "Little England beyond Wales").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

A study of a diverse sample of 2,039 individuals from the United Kingdom allowed the creation of a genetic map and the suggestion that there was a substantial migration of peoples from Europe prior to Roman times forming a strong ancestral component across England, Scotland, and Northern Ireland, but which had little impact in Wales. Wales forms a distinct genetic group, followed by a further division between north and south Wales, although there was evidence of a genetic difference between north and south Pembrokeshire as separated by the [Landsker line](/wiki/Landsker_line "Landsker line").[[42]](#cite_note-42) Speaking of these results, Professor [Peter Donnelly](/wiki/Peter_Donnelly "Peter Donnelly"), of the University of Oxford, said that the Welsh carry DNA which could be the most ancient in UK and that people from Wales are genetically relatively distinct.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Modern times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern times")]

| Year | Population of Wales |
| --- | --- |
| 1536 | 278,000 |
| 1620 | 360,000 |
| 1770 | 500,000 |
| 1801 | 587,000 |
| 1851 | 1,163,000 |
| 1911 | 2,421,000 |
| 1921 | 2,656,000 |
| 1939 | 2,487,000 |
| 1961 | 2,644,000 |
| 1991 | 2,644,000 |
| 2011 | 3,063,000[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) |

The population of Wales doubled from 587,000 in 1801 to 1,163,000 in 1851 and had reached 2,421,000 by 1911. Most of the increase came in the coal mining districts; especially [Glamorganshire](/wiki/Glamorganshire "Glamorganshire"), which grew from 71,000 in 1801 to 232,000 in 1851 and 1,122,000 in 1911.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Part of this increase can be attributed to the [demographic transition](/wiki/Demographic_transition "Demographic transition") seen in most industrialising countries during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), as death rates dropped and birth rates remained steady. However, there was also a large-scale migration into Wales during the Industrial Revolution. The English were the most numerous group, but there were also considerable numbers of Irish; and smaller numbers of other ethnic groups,[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) including [Italians](/wiki/Welsh_Italians "Welsh Italians") migrated to South Wales.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Wales received other immigration from various parts of the British [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") in the 20th century, and [African-Caribbean](/wiki/Black_British "Black British") and [Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian") communities immigrated particularly to urban Wales.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

In the 2021 Census, 0.12% (71,440) of the usual resident population of England and Wales identified as Gypsy or Irish Traveller. Of these, 94.9% (67,815) lived in England and 5.1% (3,630) lived in Wales.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The most common identity amongst people without a UK identity in Wales was Polish (0.7% of usual residents), followed by Irish (0.3%), Romanian (0.2%) and Indian (0.2%).[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### 2001 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2001 census")]

In 2001, it is uncertain how many people in Wales considered themselves to be of Welsh ethnicity; the [2001 UK census](/wiki/United_Kingdom_census,_2001 "United Kingdom census, 2001") did not offer 'Welsh' as an option; respondents had to use a box marked "Other". Ninety-six per cent of the population of Wales thus described themselves as being [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British").[[53]](#cite_note-censusresults-53) Controversy surrounding the method of determining ethnicity began as early as 2000, when it was revealed that respondents in Scotland and Northern Ireland would be able to tick a box describing themselves as of Scottish or of Irish ethnicity, an option not available for Welsh or English respondents.[[54]](#cite_note-censusequality-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Prior to the census, Plaid Cymru backed a petition calling for the inclusion of a Welsh tick-box and for the National Assembly to have primary law-making powers and its own [National Statistics Office](/wiki/National_Statistics_Office "National Statistics Office").[[54]](#cite_note-censusequality-54)

In the absence of a Welsh tick-box, the only tick-boxes available were 'white-British,' 'Irish', or 'other'.[[54]](#cite_note-censusequality-54) The Scottish parliament insisted that a Scottish ethnicity tick-box be included in the census in Scotland, and with this inclusion as many as 88.11% claimed Scottish ethnicity.[[56]](#cite_note-56) Critics argued that a higher proportion of respondents would have described themselves as of Welsh ethnicity had a Welsh tick-box been made available. Additional criticism was levelled at the timing of the census, which was taken in the middle of the [2001 United Kingdom foot-and-mouth crisis](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_foot-and-mouth_crisis "2001 United Kingdom foot-and-mouth crisis"). Organisers said that this had not affected the results.[[53]](#cite_note-censusresults-53) The foot-and-mouth crisis delayed the [2001 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election"); the first time since the Second World War that any event had postponed an election.

In the census, 14% of the population took the 'extra step' to write in that they were of Welsh ethnicity.[[57]](#cite_note-Welsh_write_in-57) The highest percentage of those identifying as of Welsh ethnicity was recorded in [Gwynedd](/wiki/Gwynedd "Gwynedd") (at 27%), followed by [Carmarthenshire](/wiki/Carmarthenshire "Carmarthenshire") (23%), [Ceredigion](/wiki/Ceredigion "Ceredigion") (22%) and the [Isle of Anglesey](/wiki/Isle_of_Anglesey "Isle of Anglesey") (19%).[[57]](#cite_note-Welsh_write_in-57) Among respondents between 16 and 74 years of age, those claiming Welsh ethnicity were predominantly in professional and managerial occupations.[[57]](#cite_note-Welsh_write_in-57)

### 2011 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2011 census")]

In advance of the 2011 UK Census, the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") (ONS) launched a census consultation exercise. They received replies from 28 different Welsh organisations and a large proportion of these referred to Welsh ethnicity, language or identity.[[58]](#cite_note-WMail2009-58)

For the first time ever in British census history the 2011 Census gave the opportunity for people to describe their identity as Welsh or English. A 'dress rehearsal' of the Census was carried out on the Welsh island of [Anglesey](/wiki/Anglesey "Anglesey") because of its rural nature *and* its high numbers of Welsh speakers.[[58]](#cite_note-WMail2009-58)
The Census, taken on 27 March 2011, asked a number of questions relating to nationality and national identity, including *What is your country of birth?* and *How would you describe your national identity?* (for the first time 'Welsh' and 'English' were included as options), *What is your ethnic group?* ('White Welsh/English/Scottish/Northern Irish/British' was an option) and *Can you understand, speak, read or write Welsh?*.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

As of the 2011 census in Wales, 66 per cent (2.0 million) of residents reported a Welsh national identity (either on its own or combined with other identities). Of these, 218,000 responded that they had Welsh and British national identity. Just under 17 per cent (519,000) of people in Wales considered themselves to have a British national identity only. Most residents of Wales (96 per cent, 2.9 million) reported at least one national identity of English, Welsh, Scottish, Northern Irish, or British.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Surveys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Surveys")]

A survey published in 2001, by the Centre for Research into Elections and Social Trends at Oxford University (sample size 1161), found that 14.6 per cent of respondents described themselves as British, not Welsh; 8.3 per cent saw themselves as more British than Welsh; 39.0 per cent described themselves as equally Welsh and British; 20.2 per cent saw themselves as more Welsh than British; and 17.9 per cent described themselves as Welsh, not British.[[61]](#cite_note-CREST_Minority_Nationalism-61)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Religion")]

See also: [Religion in Wales](/wiki/Religion_in_Wales "Religion in Wales")

Forms of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Wales "Christianity in Wales") have dominated religious life in what is now Wales for more than 1,400 years.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) Most Welsh [people of faith](/wiki/People_of_faith "People of faith") are affiliated with the [Church in Wales](/wiki/Church_in_Wales "Church in Wales") or other [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") such as the [Presbyterian Church of Wales](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_Wales "Presbyterian Church of Wales")[[64]](#cite_note-64) or [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). Wales has a long tradition of [nonconformism](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") and [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"). Some Welsh people are affiliated with either [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") or [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"). In the 2001, around 7,000 classified themselves as following "other religions", including a reconstructed form of [Druidism](/wiki/Druidism "Druidism"), which was the pre-Christian religion of Wales (not to be confused with the Druids of the [Gorsedd](/wiki/Gorsedd "Gorsedd") at the National [Eisteddfod](/wiki/Eisteddfod "Eisteddfod") of Wales). Approximately one third of the population, some 980,000 people, profess no religious faith whatsoever.[[65]](#cite_note-stats-65)

The census showed that slightly fewer than 10% of the Welsh population are regular [church](/wiki/Church_in_Wales "Church in Wales") or chapel goers (a slightly smaller proportion than in England or Scotland), although about 58% of the population see themselves as Christian in some form. Judaism has quite a long history in Wales, with a Jewish community recorded in [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea "Swansea") from around 1730. In August 1911, during a period of public order and industrial disputes, Jewish shops across the [South Wales coalfield](/wiki/South_Wales_coalfield "South Wales coalfield") were damaged by mobs. Since that time the Jewish population of that area, which reached a peak of 4,000–5,000 in 1913, has declined; only [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff") has retained a sizeable Jewish population, of about 2000 in the 2001 Census. The largest non-Christian faith in Wales is Islam, with about 22,000 members in 2001 served by about 40 mosques, following the [first mosque](/wiki/Masjid-e-Abu_Hurairah "Masjid-e-Abu Hurairah") established in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff"). A college for training clerics has been established at [Llanybydder](/wiki/Llanybydder "Llanybydder") in [West Wales](/wiki/West_Wales "West Wales"). Islam arrived in Wales in the mid 19th century, and it is thought that Cardiff's [Yemeni](/wiki/Yemenis_in_the_United_Kingdom "Yemenis in the United Kingdom") community is Britain's oldest Muslim community, established when the city was one of the world's largest coal exporting ports. [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") each have about 5,000 adherents in Wales, with the rural county of [Ceredigion](/wiki/Ceredigion "Ceredigion") being the centre of Welsh Buddhism. [Govinda](/wiki/Govinda "Govinda")'s temple and restaurant, run by the [Hare Krishnas](/wiki/International_Society_for_Krishna_Consciousness "International Society for Krishna Consciousness") in [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea "Swansea"), is a focal point for many Welsh Hindus. There are about 2,000 [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") in Wales, with the first purpose-built [gurdwara](/wiki/Gurdwara "Gurdwara") opened in the [Riverside](/wiki/Riverside,_Cardiff "Riverside, Cardiff") area of Cardiff in 1989.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Sabbatarian [temperance movement](/wiki/Temperance_movement "Temperance movement") was also historically strong among the Welsh; the sale of alcohol was prohibited on Sundays in Wales by the [Sunday Closing (Wales) Act 1881](/wiki/Sunday_Closing_(Wales)_Act_1881 "Sunday Closing (Wales) Act 1881") – the first legislation specifically issued for Wales since the Middle Ages. From the early 1960s, local council areas were permitted to hold referendums every seven years to determine whether they should be "wet" or "dry" on Sundays: most of the industrialised areas in the east and south went "wet" immediately, and by the 1980s the last district, Dwyfor in the northwest, went wet; since then there have been no more Sunday-closing referendums.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Despite Christianity dominating Wales, more ancient traditions persisted. In 1874 it was reported as common for an officiant to walk in front of the coffin with a horse's skull, which may be a tradition linked with the [Mari Lwyd](/wiki/Mari_Lwyd "Mari Lwyd") tradition.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

## Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Language")]

Main articles: [Welsh language](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"), [History of the Welsh language](/wiki/History_of_the_Welsh_language "History of the Welsh language"), and [Welsh English](/wiki/Welsh_English "Welsh English")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Welsh_speakers_in_the_2011_census.png/250px-Welsh_speakers_in_the_2011_census.png)](/wiki/File:Welsh_speakers_in_the_2011_census.png)

The proportion of respondents in the 2011 census who said they could speak Welsh

The Welsh language is in the [Insular Celtic](/wiki/Insular_Celtic "Insular Celtic") family; historically spoken throughout Wales, with its predecessor [Common Brittonic](/wiki/Common_Brittonic "Common Brittonic") once spoken throughout most of the island of Great Britain. Prior to the 20th century, large numbers of Welsh people spoke only Welsh, with little or no fluent knowledge of English.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Welsh remains the predominant language in parts of Wales, particularly in North Wales and parts of West Wales.

According to the 2001 census the number of Welsh speakers in Wales increased for the first time in 100 years, with 20.5% of a population of over 2.9 million claiming fluency in Welsh. In addition, 28% of the population of Wales claimed to understand Welsh. The census revealed that the increase was most significant in urban areas, such as Cardiff with an increase from 6.6% in 1991 to 10.9% in 2001, and [Rhondda Cynon Taf](/wiki/Rhondda_Cynon_Taf "Rhondda Cynon Taf") with an increase from 9% in 1991 to 12.3% in 2001. However, the proportion of Welsh speakers declined in [Gwynedd](/wiki/Gwynedd "Gwynedd") from 72.1% in 1991 to 68.7% in 2001, to 65.4% in 2011 and 64.4% in 2021. Similarly, in [Ceredigion](/wiki/Ceredigion "Ceredigion") the percentage fell from 59.1% in 1991 to 51.8% in 2001, to 47.3% in 2011 and to 45.3% in 2021. Ceredigion saw a 19.5% influx of new residents between 1991 and 2001.[[53]](#cite_note-censusresults-53)

The decline in Welsh speakers in much of rural Wales is attributable to non-Welsh-speaking residents moving to North Wales, driving up property prices above what locals may afford, according to former [Gwynedd](/wiki/Gwynedd "Gwynedd") county councillor [Seimon Glyn](/w/index.php?title=Seimon_Glyn&action=edit&redlink=1 "Seimon Glyn (page does not exist)") of [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru"), whose controversial comments in 2001 focused attention on the issue.[[53]](#cite_note-censusresults-53) As many as a third of all properties in Gwynedd are bought by people from outside Wales.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The issue of locals being priced out of the local housing market is common to many rural communities throughout Britain, but in Wales the added dimension of language complicates the issue, as many new residents do not learn the Welsh language.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

A Plaid Cymru taskforce headed by Dafydd Wigley recommended land should be allocated for affordable local housing, called for grants for locals to buy houses, and recommended that [council tax](/wiki/Council_tax "Council tax") on holiday homes should double.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

However, the same census shows that 25% of residents were born outside Wales. The number of Welsh speakers in other places in Britain is uncertain, but there are significant numbers in the main cities, and there are speakers along the [Welsh-English border](/wiki/Wales-England_border "Wales-England border").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Even among Welsh speakers, very few people speak only Welsh, with nearly all being [bilingual](/wiki/Bilingual "Bilingual") in English. However, a large number of Welsh speakers are more comfortable expressing themselves in Welsh than in English. Some prefer to speak English in South Wales or the urbanised areas and Welsh in the North or in rural areas. A speaker's choice of language can vary according to the subject domain (known in linguistics as [code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Due to an increase in Welsh-language nursery education, recent census data reveals a reversal of decades of linguistic decline: there are now more Welsh speakers under five years of age than over 60. For many young people in Wales, the acquisition of Welsh is a gateway to better careers, according to research from the [Welsh Language Board](/wiki/Welsh_Language_Board "Welsh Language Board") and Careers Wales.[[71]](#cite_note-DewisDa-71) The Welsh Government identified media as one of six areas likely to experience greater demand for Welsh speakers:[[71]](#cite_note-DewisDa-71) the sector is Wales's third-largest revenue earner.

Although Welsh is a [minority language](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language"), and thus threatened by the dominance of English, support for the language grew during the second half of the 20th century, along with the rise of [Welsh nationalism](/wiki/Welsh_nationalism "Welsh nationalism") in the form of groups such as the political party [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") and [Cymdeithas yr Iaith Gymraeg](/wiki/Cymdeithas_yr_Iaith_Gymraeg "Cymdeithas yr Iaith Gymraeg") (Welsh Language Society). The language is used in the bilingual [Welsh Parliament](/wiki/Welsh_Parliament "Welsh Parliament") (Senedd) and entered on its records, with English translation. The high cost of translation from English to Welsh has proved controversial.[[72]](#cite_note-72) In the past the rules of the [British Parliament](/wiki/British_Parliament "British Parliament") forbade the use of Welsh in any proceedings. Only English was allowed as the only language all members were assumed to speak.[[73]](#cite_note-73) In 2017, the UK government agreed to support the use of Welsh in the Welsh Grand Committee, although not in parliamentary debate in the house outside of this committee.[[74]](#cite_note-74) In 2018 Welsh was used in the grand committee for the first time.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Welsh as a [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language") is largely concentrated in the less urban north and west of Wales, principally [Gwynedd](/wiki/Gwynedd "Gwynedd"), inland Conwy and [Denbighshire](/wiki/Denbighshire "Denbighshire"), northern and south-western [Powys](/wiki/Powys "Powys"), the [Isle of Anglesey](/wiki/Isle_of_Anglesey "Isle of Anglesey"), [Carmarthenshire](/wiki/Carmarthenshire "Carmarthenshire"), North [Pembrokeshire](/wiki/Pembrokeshire "Pembrokeshire"), [Ceredigion](/wiki/Ceredigion "Ceredigion"), and parts of western [Glamorgan](/wiki/Glamorgan "Glamorgan"), although first-language and other fluent speakers can be found throughout Wales. However, Cardiff is now home to an urban Welsh-speaking population (both from other parts of Wales and from the growing Welsh-medium schools of Cardiff itself) due to the centralisation and concentration of national resources and organisations in the capital.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For some, speaking Welsh is an important part of their Welsh identity. Parts of the culture are strongly connected to the language — notably the Eisteddfod tradition, poetry and aspects of folk music and dance. Wales also has a strong tradition of poetry in the English language.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Patagonian Welsh](/wiki/Patagonian_Welsh "Patagonian Welsh") (Cymraeg y Wladfa) is a dialect of the [Welsh language](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") which is spoken in [Y Wladfa](/wiki/Y_Wladfa "Y Wladfa") in the [Argentine](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") region, [Patagonia](/wiki/Patagonia "Patagonia").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## National symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: National symbols")]

Main article: [National symbols of Wales](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Wales "National symbols of Wales")

* The [Flag of Wales](/wiki/Flag_of_Wales "Flag of Wales") (*Baner Cymru*) incorporates the [red](/wiki/Red "Red") [dragon](/wiki/Dragon "Dragon") (*Y Ddraig Goch*), a popular symbol of Wales and the Welsh people, along with the [Tudor](/wiki/Tudor_dynasty "Tudor dynasty") colours of green and white. It was used by Henry VII at the [Battle of Bosworth Field](/wiki/Battle_of_Bosworth_Field "Battle of Bosworth Field") in 1485, after which it was carried in state to [St. Paul's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_Cathedral "St. Paul's Cathedral"). The red dragon was then included in the Tudor royal arms to signify their Welsh descent. It was officially recognised as the Welsh national flag in 1959. Since the British [Union Flag](/wiki/Union_Flag "Union Flag") does not have any Welsh representation, the Flag of Wales has become very popular.
* The [Flag of Saint David](/wiki/Flag_of_Saint_David "Flag of Saint David") is sometimes used as an alternative to the national flag, and is flown on [Saint David's Day](/wiki/Saint_David%27s_Day "Saint David's Day").
* The [dragon](/wiki/Dragon "Dragon"), part of the national flag design, is also a popular Welsh symbol. The oldest recorded use of the dragon to symbolise Wales is from the *[Historia Brittonum](/wiki/Historia_Brittonum "Historia Brittonum")*, written around 820, but it is popularly supposed to have been the battle standard of [King Arthur](/wiki/King_Arthur "King Arthur") and other ancient Celtic leaders. Following the annexation of Wales by England, the dragon was used as a supporter in the English monarch's coat of arms.
* Both the [daffodil](/wiki/Daffodil "Daffodil") and the [leek](/wiki/Leek "Leek") are symbols of Wales. The origin of the leek can be traced back to the 16th century and the daffodil, encouraged by [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George"), became popular in the 19th century.[[76]](#cite_note-76) This may be due to confusion of the Welsh for leek, *cenhinen*, and that for daffodil, *cenhinen Bedr* or St. Peter's leek. Both are worn as symbols by the Welsh on [Saint David's Day](/wiki/Saint_David%27s_Day "Saint David's Day"), 1 March.
* The [Prince of Wales' feathers](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales%27_feathers "Prince of Wales' feathers"), the heraldic badge of the [Prince of Wales](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales "Prince of Wales"), is sometimes adapted by Welsh bodies for use in Wales. The symbolism is explained on the article for [Edward, the Black Prince](/wiki/Edward,_the_Black_Prince#Emblem "Edward, the Black Prince"), who was the first Prince of Wales to bear the emblem. The [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") uses such a design for its own badge.

## Welsh emigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Welsh_people&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Welsh emigration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Flag_of_the_Welsh_colony_in_Patagonia.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Welsh_colony_in_Patagonia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Welsh_colony_in_Patagonia.svg)

Flag of the city of [Puerto Madryn](/wiki/Puerto_Madryn "Puerto Madryn"), Argentina, inspired by the [Flag of Wales](/wiki/Flag_of_Wales "Flag of Wales"), owing to the Welsh immigration

There has been migration from Wales to the rest of Britain throughout its history. During the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") thousands of Welsh people migrated, for example, to [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") and [Ashton-in-Makerfield](/wiki/Ashton-in-Makerfield "Ashton-in-Makerfield").[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) As a result, some people from England, Scotland and Ireland have Welsh surnames.[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/John_Adams_Portrait.jpg/250px-John_Adams_Portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Adams_Portrait.jpg)

[John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams"), the second President of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") (1797–1801), whose paternal great-grandfather David Adams, born and bred at Fferm Penybanc, [Llanboidy](/wiki/Llanboidy "Llanboidy"), [Carmarthenshire](/wiki/Carmarthenshire "Carmarthenshire"), emigrated from Wales in 1675[[83]](#cite_note-83)

Welsh settlers moved to other parts of Europe, concentrated in certain areas. During the late 19th and early 20th centuries, a small wave of contract miners from Wales arrived in Northern France; the centres of Welsh-French population are in coal mining towns, and particularly the French department of [Pas-de-Calais](/wiki/Pas-de-Calais "Pas-de-Calais") along with miners from many other countries. They tended to cluster in communities around their churches.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

Settlers from Wales (and later Patagonian Welsh) arrived in [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador") in the early 19th century, and founded towns in [Labrador](/wiki/Labrador "Labrador")'s coast region; in 1819, the ship *Albion* left [Cardigan](/wiki/Cardigan,_Ceredigion "Cardigan, Ceredigion") for [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"), carrying Welsh settlers to Canada; on board were 27 Cardiganian families, many of whom were farmers.[[85]](#cite_note-85) In 1852, [Thomas Benbow Phillips](/wiki/Thomas_Benbow_Phillips "Thomas Benbow Phillips") of [Tregaron](/wiki/Tregaron "Tregaron") established a settlement of about 100 Welsh people in the state of [Rio Grande do Sul](/wiki/Rio_Grande_do_Sul "Rio Grande do Sul") in Brazil.

Internationally Welsh people have emigrated, in relatively small numbers (in proportion to population, Irish emigration to the US may have been 26 times greater than Welsh emigration),[[86]](#cite_note-Australia-86) to many countries, including the US (in particular, [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania")), Canada and [Y Wladfa](/wiki/Y_Wladfa "Y Wladfa") in [Patagonia](/wiki/Patagonia "Patagonia"), Argentina.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) [Jackson County, Ohio](/wiki/Jackson_County,_Ohio "Jackson County, Ohio") was sometimes referred to as "Little Wales", and one of several communities where Welsh was widely spoken. There was a Welsh language press but by the late 1940s, the last Welsh language newspaper, *y Drych* began to publish in English.[[90]](#cite_note-90) [Malad City](/wiki/Malad_City,_Idaho "Malad City, Idaho") in [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho"), which began as a Welsh [Mormon](/wiki/Mormon "Mormon") settlement, lays claim to a greater proportion of inhabitants of Welsh descent than anywhere outside Wales itself.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Malad's local High School is known as the "Malad Dragons", and flies the [Welsh Flag](/wiki/Welsh_Flag "Welsh Flag") as its school colours.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Welsh people have also settled in New Zealand and Australia.[[86]](#cite_note-Australia-86)[[93]](#cite_note-93)

Around 1.75 million Americans report themselves to have Welsh ancestry, as did 458,705 Canadians in [Canada's 2011 census](/wiki/Canada_2011_Census "Canada 2011 Census").[[2]](#cite_note-usa-2)[[4]](#cite_note-Welsh_Canadians-4) This compares with 2.9 million people living in Wales (as of the [2001 census](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Census_2001 "United Kingdom Census 2001")).[[94]](#cite_note-estimate-94)

There is no known evidence which would objectively support the legend that the [Mandan](/wiki/Mandan "Mandan"), a Native American tribe of the central United States, are Welsh emigrants who reached North America under Prince [Madog](/wiki/Madog "Madog") in 1170.[[95]](#cite_note-straightdope-95)

The Ukrainian city of [Donetsk](/wiki/Donetsk "Donetsk") was founded in 1869 by a Welsh businessman, [John Hughes](/wiki/John_Hughes_(businessman) "John Hughes (businessman)") (an engineer from [Merthyr Tydfil](/wiki/Merthyr_Tydfil "Merthyr Tydfil")) who constructed a steel plant and several [coal mines](/wiki/Coal_mining "Coal mining") in the region; the town was thus named *Yuzovka* (Юзовка) in recognition of his role in its founding ("Yuz" being a Russian or Ukrainian approximation of Hughes).[[96]](#cite_note-96)

Former Australian Prime Minister [Julia Gillard](/wiki/Julia_Gillard "Julia Gillard") was born in Barry, Wales. After she suffered from [bronchopneumonia](/wiki/Bronchopneumonia "Bronchopneumonia") as a child, her parents were advised that it would aid her recovery to live in a warmer climate. This led the family to migrate to Australia in 1966, settling in Adelaide.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
